% ---- Scope note (goes in the Subjects paragraph / a footnote) --------------
% The study covers the SEVEN largest-adoption Solidity protocols that ship a runnable
% Foundry test suite (the empirical gas methodology of Section 5 requires one): Aave V3,
% Lido, Gearbox V3, Morpho Blue, OpenZeppelin Contracts, Seaport, and Uniswap V4 Core.
% Three otherwise-eligible protocols were excluded for lack of a runnable Foundry gas
% suite and are discussed under Threats to Validity: ERC-4337 account-abstraction
% (Hardhat only, no foundry.toml), Compound III/Comet (fork-only tests, no local RPC),
% and Fluid (47 mainnet-fork test sites).

\paragraph{RQ1 (Applicability).}
gasopt parsed, rewrote, and recompiled all seven subjects without any manual edit to
contract source. The only per-project preparation was standard build setup that the
projects' own CI also performs --- installing dependencies (\texttt{npm ci} for
OpenZeppelin, whose account-abstraction tests read an EntryPoint predeploy from
\texttt{node\_modules}) and materialising a \texttt{remappings.txt} where the repository
relies on Foundry's implicit \texttt{lib/} resolution (Seaport). No subject required a
change to a precondition or a transform to get through the pipeline. Table~\ref{tab:rq1}
reports, per subject, the number of production files gasopt rewrote and whether an
end-to-end empirical gas measurement was obtained. Two subjects could not be gas-measured
under their \emph{project-default} compiler profile because that profile pins an infeasible
optimiser-run count (Uniswap V4 Core at \texttt{optimizer\_runs}~$=44{,}444{,}444$, Seaport
at $2^{32}-1$), whose whole-suite compile exhausts memory; for these we measure at
\texttt{optimizer\_runs}~$=200$ (the same setting as the RQ5 factorial), noted explicitly
rather than reported as a project-default number. No subject failed at the parse/rewrite
stage.
% Table rq1: from analysis/tables/rq1_applicability.csv
%  aave-v3-origin 186 total / 66 optimised / 0 skipped / gas OK
%  core (Lido)    128 / 50 / 3 skipped(1 compile,2 verify) / gas OK
%  core-v3 (Gearbox) 69 / 19 / 0 / gas OK
%  morpho-blue    17 / 2 / 0 / gas OK
%  openzeppelin   248 / 39 / 0 / gas OK (library-embedded)
%  seaport        <<PENDING>>
%  v4-core        46 / 2 / 0 / gas at runs=200 (project-default OOM)

\paragraph{RQ2 (Rule coverage).}
Across the seven subjects, \textbf{14 of the 24 rules} that are active without
\texttt{-{}-closed-world} fired at least once on production code (struct-packing and the
promotion half of visibility require the closed-world assumption and were not enabled).
Figure~\ref{fig:rq2} gives the per-rule, per-protocol counts. The most frequently applied
were \textsf{avoid-zero-init} (260 rewrites across 5 protocols),
\textsf{pre-increment} (111 across 3), \textsf{payable-constructor} (73 across 6, the most
widely-applicable rule), \textsf{unchecked} (58 across 4), \textsf{require-error} (47
across 2), and \textsf{delete-storage} (33 across 5). Several catalogued rules never matched
on any subject (\textsf{cache-storage}, \textsf{loop-invariant}, \textsf{use-immutable},
\textsf{use-constant}, \textsf{loop-fusion}, \textsf{remove-const-loop},
\textsf{factor-loops}, \textsf{single-line-swap}), a finding in itself: the loop- and
caching-oriented rules that dominate manual gas-golfing guides rarely have a
precondition-satisfying site in modern, already-audited production code.
% from analysis/tables/rq2_rule_coverage.csv (+ seaport pending)

\paragraph{RQ3 (Effectiveness).}
Table~\ref{tab:rq3} reports, per subject, the change in summed deployment gas and bytecode
size over its production deployables, measured empirically through the project's own suite.
The effect is real but modest, and it is concentrated in subjects with many concrete
deployable contracts: Aave V3 shows the largest absolute saving,
$-788{,}600$ deployment gas ($-0.98\%$) and $-5{,}319$ bytes ($-1.10\%$) over 34 production
contracts; Lido $-1.34\%$; Gearbox $-0.012\%$; Morpho $-0.004\%$. Median per-function
runtime-gas change is essentially zero. The reason is visible in RQ5: on a modern compiler
(solc $\geq0.8.2x$ with the optimiser on) many source-level rewrites in the catalogue --
zero-initialisation removal, pre-increment, short-circuiting -- are already captured by the
compiler, so their marginal source-level effect is small. OpenZeppelin is a pure library:
its contracts are never deployed standalone, so a Foundry gas report attributes their cost
to the test/mock contracts that embed them; we therefore report OpenZeppelin's effect at a
\emph{library-embedded} scope ($-1{,}652$ gas over 18 embedding contracts), never conflated
with a standalone production-deploy saving.
% from analysis/tables/rq3_effectiveness_per_protocol.csv (+ seaport pending)

\paragraph{RQ4 (Correctness in practice).}
This is the central safety result. Across every subject that was gas-measured, the
differential Foundry measurement reported \textbf{zero} \texttt{skippedAfterRewriteOnly}
tests: not a single test that passed on the pre-rewrite baseline began failing after gasopt
rewrote the code. No protocol's own suite revealed a behavioural regression that the
preconditions and the verify gate did not already prevent. The preconditions were, moreover,
actively conservative rather than vacuous: they \emph{declined} 390 candidate rewrite sites
in OpenZeppelin, 243 in Aave, 238 in Lido, 41 in Gearbox, and 3 in Morpho -- sites that
matched a rule's shape but failed its precondition and were left untouched. Where a test was
non-deterministic under a fixed seed (e.g.\ 52 gas-report entries in Morpho's fuzzed suite),
it was flagged and excluded from the before/after comparison rather than mistaken for a
saving.
% from analysis/tables/rq4_correctness.csv (+ seaport pending)

\paragraph{RQ5 (Comparative standing).}
We measured the full $2\times2$ factorial (code: original~$\times$~gasopt) $\times$
(compiler: standard~$\times$~via-IR) at a fixed seed and \texttt{optimizer\_runs}~$=200$, so
that via-IR is the only factor separating the two compiler columns (Table~\ref{tab:rq5},
Figure~\ref{fig:rq5}). Four findings follow, and we report them as four separate questions
rather than a ``gasopt vs.\ the compiler'' contest, because the two act at different levels
and compose:
\begin{itemize}
\item \emph{The compiler alone} (A$\to$B) delivers the large deployment-gas reductions where
it applies: $-26.7\%$ on Morpho, $-13.0\%$ on Uniswap V4, $-0.97\%$ on OpenZeppelin.
\item \emph{gasopt alone}, in the standard-optimiser configuration most common in production
(A$\to$C), delivers the smaller source-level savings of RQ3: $-0.98\%$ (Aave), $-0.72\%$
(Lido), and fractions of a percent elsewhere.
\item \emph{gasopt under via-IR} (B$\to$D) remains negative wherever via-IR is available
($-0.005\%$ Morpho, $-0.002\%$ Uniswap V4, $-0.012\%$ OpenZeppelin --- OpenZeppelin in fact
saves \emph{more} under via-IR). The source-level savings are not subsumed by the compiler's
most aggressive mode; they persist on top of it. This confirms at whole-protocol scale what
our earlier work~\cite{ensuringgasoptimisation} could only show for three isolated rules:
these refactorings address opportunities that lie beyond the compiler's scope.
\item \emph{The best practical configuration} (A$\to$D) is via-IR with gasopt applied, and it
is dominated by via-IR ($-26.75\%$ Morpho, $-13.03\%$ Uniswap V4). The concrete recommendation
to a developer is therefore: enable via-IR for the large win, and additionally run gasopt for
a small, orthogonal, regression-free further reduction.
\end{itemize}
Where the two overlap rather than compose we say so: on OpenZeppelin the compiler already
captures most of what gasopt's rewrites target, so gasopt's standalone effect is small --- a
legitimate result, not a failure of the tool.
